q: Retrieve all employees and their department name. If an employee does not belong to any department, show their details with a NULL department.
explain: We need to preserve all rows from the Left table (employees), regardless of whether there is a corresponding row in the departments table. A LEFT JOIN is ideal here.

q: Show only the employees who are actively assigned to a department. Exclude employees without a department and departments without employees.
explain: We want the intersection: only rows where the employee has a department_id and there is a matching department. This requires an INNER JOIN.

q: List all departments and the names of employees working in them. If a department has no employees, still display the department name.
explain: To preserve all departments, we place 'departments' on the left and use a LEFT JOIN (or place it on the right and use a RIGHT JOIN).

q: Display a complete list of all employees and all departments. Include employees who don't have a department and departments that have no employees.
explain: We must preserve all records from both tables. Rows that don't match on either side will be filled with NULLs. This requires a FULL OUTER JOIN.

q: Generate a grid of all employees paired against every project in the system, so we can check all possible project assignments.
explain: We want a Cartesian product: every employee row combined with every project row, without any matching condition. This requires a CROSS JOIN.

q: Find all employees along with the name of their manager. If an employee doesn't have a manager (e.g. the CEO), still display the employee.
explain: Since manager details are in the same employee table, we join the table to itself using aliases. A LEFT JOIN ensures employees without a manager are kept.

q: Find employees who are earning the exact same salary as someone else in the company. Exclude matching an employee with themselves.
explain: We join the employee table to itself on matching salaries. Using 'e1.id < e2.id' avoids self-matching and filters out duplicate pairs (A-B and B-A).

q: List all customers and the dates of their orders. Include customers who have never placed an order.
explain: All customers must be preserved. Customers with no orders will have a NULL order_date, indicating a LEFT JOIN.

q: Find only the customers who have placed at least one order, returning their name and the order ID.
explain: Only matching customer-order pairs are needed. Customers with no orders are omitted. This requires an INNER JOIN.

q: List all orders along with the customer email. Include orders that might not be linked to a registered customer account (e.g., guest checkouts).
explain: We want to preserve all orders, regardless of customer existence. Placing orders on the left and using a LEFT JOIN is correct.

q: List all students and the classes they are enrolled in. Exclude students who are not registered for any classes and empty classes.
sql:
SELECT s.name AS student_name, c.title AS course_title
FROM students s
INNER JOIN enrollments e ON s.id = e.student_id
INNER JOIN courses c ON e.course_id = c.id;
explain: This is a three-table join. Since we want only active student-course pairings, we use INNER JOINs across all connections.

q: List all students and any classes they have enrolled in. Include students who have not registered for any classes.
sql:
SELECT s.name AS student_name, c.title AS course_title
FROM students s
LEFT JOIN enrollments e ON s.id = e.student_id
LEFT JOIN courses c ON e.course_id = c.id;
explain: We must preserve all students. We LEFT JOIN enrollments, and then LEFT JOIN courses to keep the student records intact even if they have 0 registrations.

q: Show all courses and the students enrolled in them. Include courses that currently have zero students registered.
sql:
SELECT c.title AS course_title, s.name AS student_name
FROM courses c
LEFT JOIN enrollments e ON c.id = e.course_id
LEFT JOIN students s ON e.student_id = s.id;
explain: To preserve all courses (even empty ones), courses must be the primary (leftmost) table in a chain of LEFT JOINs.

q: List all authors and the titles of books they've written. If an author hasn't written any books, show their name with NULL as the title.
explain: Preserves all authors. Authors without matching books in the books table will show NULL for the book title.

q: List all books and their publisher's name. Include books that do not have a publisher assigned yet.
explain: We want to preserve all books, even if they lack a publisher. Place books on the left and use a LEFT JOIN.

q: Retrieve all publishers and their books. Include publishers who haven't published any books yet.
explain: All publishers must be preserved. Therefore, publishers is the Left table joined to books using a LEFT JOIN.

q: Match authors and publishers where they reside in the same city. Only return matches.
explain: A standard inner join that matches authors to publishers on the condition of sharing the same city.

q: Show all flights and their arrival airport codes. Include flights that have not landed (arrival airport is NULL/unassigned).
explain: Flights is the primary table that needs preservation. Unlanded flights will have a NULL airport code, hence a LEFT JOIN.

q: List all airports and the flight numbers landing there. Include airports that currently have no incoming flights.
explain: We preserve all airports, making it the Left table. We LEFT JOIN flights to ensure airports with no flights are displayed.

q: Show all flight schedules and passenger names, including empty flights and passengers who haven't booked any flights.
sql:
SELECT f.flight_number, p.name AS passenger_name
FROM bookings b
FULL OUTER JOIN flights f ON b.flight_id = f.id
FULL OUTER JOIN passengers p ON b.passenger_id = p.id;
explain: Both flights (possibly empty) and passengers (possibly without bookings) must be preserved. We use a FULL OUTER JOIN chain.

q: Find all doctor-patient pairs that have a matching appointment. Omit doctors with no patients and patients with no appointments.
sql:
SELECT d.name AS doctor_name, p.name AS patient_name
FROM appointments a
INNER JOIN doctors d ON a.doctor_id = d.id
INNER JOIN patients p ON a.patient_id = p.id;
explain: We only want active appointments. Therefore, we use INNER JOINs to link appointments to doctors and patients.

q: Show all doctors and their patient appointments, including doctors who have not scheduled any appointments.
sql:
SELECT d.name AS doctor_name, a.appointment_date, p.name AS patient_name
FROM doctors d
LEFT JOIN appointments a ON d.id = a.doctor_id
LEFT JOIN patients p ON a.patient_id = p.id;
explain: Doctors is the primary table. We LEFT JOIN appointments and patients to preserve doctors with zero appointments.

q: Show all patients and their scheduled doctors, including patients who have not booked any appointments.
sql:
SELECT p.name AS patient_name, d.name AS doctor_name
FROM patients p
LEFT JOIN appointments a ON p.id = a.patient_id
LEFT JOIN doctors d ON a.doctor_id = d.id;
explain: Patients must be preserved. We start with patients on the left and LEFT JOIN appointments and doctors.

q: List all products and their categories. If a product does not belong to a category, show NULL for the category name.
explain: Preserves all products. Uncategorized products will display NULL for category_name.

q: Find categories that have products. Exclude empty categories and uncategorized products.
explain: Strict matching between categories and products. Standard INNER JOIN.

q: List all categories, and display the products in them. Include empty categories.
explain: Categories must be preserved, even if they contain no products. Place categories on the left of a LEFT JOIN.

q: Produce a combined list of all products and all categories, showing unmatched products and empty categories together.
explain: Preserves both tables entirely. Product without categories and categories with no products are kept. Requires a FULL OUTER JOIN.

q: Generate a combinations list of every product in the catalog with every store location.
explain: We need the Cartesian product of products and stores. A CROSS JOIN generates all possible pairs.

q: Find pairs of products in the same category that have the exact same price.
explain: A self join matches the products table with itself on category_id and price, excluding self-matches with '<' ID filter.

q: Find all users and their uploaded documents. Include users who have uploaded nothing.
explain: Keep all users (Left table) regardless of whether they have uploaded files. Uses a LEFT JOIN.

q: Show only users who have uploaded documents, along with the document filenames.
explain: We only want the intersection of users and documents (exclude document-less users). This is an INNER JOIN.

q: Retrieve all documents in the system and their uploader username. Include documents that are orphaned (have no valid user_id link).
explain: Preserve all documents. Place documents on the left and use a LEFT JOIN to link users.

q: Show all projects and the employees assigned to them. Exclude unassigned projects and employee-less projects.
sql:
SELECT p.title AS project_title, e.name AS employee_name
FROM projects p
INNER JOIN employee_projects ep ON p.id = ep.project_id
INNER JOIN employees e ON ep.employee_id = e.id;
explain: Only return active assignments by joining projects to employees via junction table using INNER JOINs.

q: Show all projects and any employees assigned to them. Include projects with no employees assigned.
sql:
SELECT p.title AS project_title, e.name AS employee_name
FROM projects p
LEFT JOIN employee_projects ep ON p.id = ep.project_id
LEFT JOIN employees e ON ep.employee_id = e.id;
explain: Preserves all projects. We LEFT JOIN the association table and the employee table to keep empty projects.

q: Retrieve all employees and their assigned projects. Include employees who are currently not assigned to any projects.
sql:
SELECT e.name AS employee_name, p.title AS project_title
FROM employees e
LEFT JOIN employee_projects ep ON e.id = ep.employee_id
LEFT JOIN projects p ON ep.project_id = p.id;
explain: Preserves all employees. We LEFT JOIN the junction table and project table to keep unassigned employees.

q: List all suppliers and the parts they supply. Include suppliers who do not supply any parts.
explain: Keep all suppliers (Left table), showing NULL part names for suppliers with no catalog parts. Requires a LEFT JOIN.

q: Show parts that have a supplier. Omit parts without an assigned supplier.
explain: Match parts to suppliers; exclude orphaned parts. Requires a standard INNER JOIN.

q: List all departments and their offices. Include offices that do not host any departments.
join: RIGHT JOIN (or LEFT JOIN with table swap)
explain: We need to preserve all offices. Placing offices on the right and using a RIGHT JOIN (or swapping and using LEFT JOIN) accomplishes this.

q: Match students and advisors who share the exact same first name. Return only matches.
explain: A standard inner join that matches students to advisors on the text condition of first name equality.

q: Compare student enrollment counts. Generate all possible pairs of courses to evaluate overlaps.
sql:
SELECT c1.title AS course1, c2.title AS course2
FROM courses c1
CROSS JOIN courses c2
WHERE c1.id < c2.id;
explain: A cross join combines all courses. The WHERE condition filters out duplicate matching and matching a course with itself.

q: List all blog posts and their comments. Include posts that have zero comments.
explain: Posts must be preserved. Comment-less posts will show NULL for comment text. This is a LEFT JOIN.

q: Show posts that have comments. Exclude posts with no comments.
explain: Strict match. Exclude post rows with no comments. Requires an INNER JOIN.

q: Show all comments and the posts they belong to. Include comments that are not associated with a post.
explain: Preserves all comments. Omitted post references will show NULL for post titles. Requires a LEFT JOIN.

q: List all authors and all articles they have co-authored. Include articles with no authors and authors with no articles.
explain: Preserves both tables entirely, making FULL OUTER JOIN the required option.

q: Find all tasks in a project. If a task has a prerequisite task, show the prerequisite task title. Include tasks without prerequisites.
explain: Since prerequisite details are within the tasks table, we join the table to itself on prereq_id = id. LEFT JOIN keeps tasks with no prerequisites.

q: Find employees and their managers. Exclude employees who don't have a manager.
explain: Self-join on employee-manager hierarchy. INNER JOIN filters out employees (like the CEO) who have no manager.

q: Generate all possible pairs of team members to form 2-person project teams.
explain: A self join with ID comparison generates unique pairings of team members without repeats.

q: List all books and their translations. Include books that have not been translated.
explain: All books are preserved. Books with no translation rows will show NULL for translation fields. Requires a LEFT JOIN.

q: Show books that have translations. Omit untranslated books.
explain: Only return records that have matching rows in both books and translations. Requires an INNER JOIN.

q: List all transaction accounts and their branches. Include branches without any registered accounts.
join: RIGHT JOIN (or LEFT JOIN with table swap)
explain: Branches must be preserved. We use a RIGHT JOIN if branches is on the right, or a LEFT JOIN if we put branches on the left.

q: List all students and advisors. Display matches where possible. Include unassigned students and advisors with no assigned students.
explain: Requires preservation of both tables (unassigned students and advisors with no students). FULL OUTER JOIN is the correct choice.

q: Find all products that have the exact same weight. Exclude matching a product with itself.
explain: Self-join on weight. Using < operator prevents duplicate combinations and matching a product to itself.

q: Create a list of all possible flight itineraries connecting our departure and arrival airports directly.
sql:
SELECT a1.code AS departure, a2.code AS arrival
FROM airports a1
CROSS JOIN airports a2
WHERE a1.id <> a2.id;
explain: A CROSS JOIN generates all possible departure-arrival combinations. The inequality filters out same-airport flights.

q: Retrieve all customer details along with their discount coupon code. Include customers who do not have a coupon.
explain: Preserves all customers. Customers with no discount coupon will show NULL. This is a LEFT JOIN.

q: Find customers who have used a discount coupon. Exclude customers without coupons.
explain: Intersection: only returns customers who hold a valid matching coupon. Requires an INNER JOIN.

q: List all discount coupons and the customers who have redeemed them. Include coupons that have not been redeemed yet.
explain: Preserves all coupons. Unredeemed coupons will show NULL for customer name. Requires a LEFT JOIN with coupons as the primary table.

q: Show all users and their profile badges. Include users with no badges and badges that haven't been earned by anyone.
sql:
SELECT u.name, b.title AS badge_title
FROM users u
FULL OUTER JOIN user_badges ub ON u.id = ub.user_id
FULL OUTER JOIN badges b ON ub.badge_id = b.id;
explain: All users (with or without badges) and all badges (earned or not) must be preserved. Requires FULL OUTER JOINs.

q: Find orders and their items. Exclude orders that contain no items (empty orders) and items not linked to an order.
sql:
SELECT o.id AS order_id, i.name AS item_name
FROM orders o
INNER JOIN order_items oi ON o.id = oi.order_id
INNER JOIN items i ON oi.item_id = i.id;
explain: Standard inner join matching order header to line items to item definitions. Discards unmatched entries.

q: Show all orders and their items. Include orders that have no items listed in the database.
sql:
SELECT o.id AS order_id, i.name AS item_name
FROM orders o
LEFT JOIN order_items oi ON o.id = oi.order_id
LEFT JOIN items i ON oi.item_id = i.id;
explain: Keep all orders. Left join to order items and item descriptions to ensure empty orders remain in the result.

q: Retrieve all items in inventory and the order IDs they appear in. Include items that have never been ordered.
explain: Preserves all inventory items. Items with no sales history show NULL order IDs, requiring a LEFT JOIN.

q: List all events and their venues. Include events without a venue assigned yet.
explain: Keeps all events. Unassigned venues show as NULL. This requires a LEFT JOIN.

q: List all venues and the events hosted in them. Include venues that have no events scheduled.
explain: Keeps all venues. Venues with zero events will have NULL event titles. Requires a LEFT JOIN starting with venues.

q: List all events and all venues, displaying matches where possible. Include events without venues and venues with no events.
explain: Requires complete preservation of both events and venues, which is the definition of a FULL OUTER JOIN.

q: Show only events that have a venue assigned, along with the venue name.
explain: Only return events that have a valid match in the venues table. Requires an INNER JOIN.

q: Find all users who reside in the same state. Exclude matching a user to themselves.
explain: Self-join on state. The ID comparison avoids self-matching and lists each unique pair once.

q: List all employees and their work office. Include offices that are empty.
join: RIGHT JOIN (or LEFT JOIN with table swap)
explain: Offices must be preserved. Using RIGHT JOIN preserves the right table (offices).

q: Show all assets and their users. Include assets with no user and users with no assets.
explain: Preserves both assets and users fully, which requires a FULL OUTER JOIN.

q: List all tasks and the employees assigned. Include tasks that are unassigned.
explain: Preserves all tasks. Unassigned tasks will show NULL for employee name. Requires a LEFT JOIN.

q: Find tasks that have been assigned. Exclude tasks without an assigned employee.
explain: Only show matching tasks and employees. Unassigned tasks are excluded. Requires an INNER JOIN.

q: Retrieve all employees and their tasks. Include employees who have no tasks assigned to them.
explain: Preserves all employees. Employees with no tasks show NULL for task_name. Requires a LEFT JOIN starting with employees.

q: List all movies and their reviews. Include movies that have not received any reviews.
explain: Movies must be preserved. Review fields for unreviewed movies will show NULL, indicating a LEFT JOIN.

q: Show only movies that have reviews, along with the reviews.
explain: Only return movies that have corresponding records in the reviews table. Requires an INNER JOIN.

q: List all reviews and the movies they belong to. Include orphaned reviews that don't match any movie in the database.
explain: All reviews must be preserved. Orphaned reviews will show NULL for movie title. Requires a LEFT JOIN starting with reviews.

q: Show all movies and all reviews, displaying mismatches on both sides.
explain: Preserves all movies (even unreviewed) and all reviews (even orphaned). Requires a FULL OUTER JOIN.

q: Generate all possible pairs of movies to create a side-by-side comparison poll.
sql:
SELECT m1.title AS movie1, m2.title AS movie2
FROM movies m1
CROSS JOIN movies m2
WHERE m1.id < m2.id;
explain: CROSS JOIN yields all movie pairings. The filter eliminates matching a movie to itself and duplicates.

q: Find movies in the database released in the same year. Exclude matching a movie to itself.
explain: Self-join on release_year. Filters eliminate self-matching and duplicate comparisons.

q: List all customers and their credit cards. Include customers who do not have a credit card.
explain: Preserves all customers. Card details for card-less customers show as NULL. Requires a LEFT JOIN.

q: Show only customers who have registered credit cards.
explain: Returns only customers with matching cards. Requires an INNER JOIN.

q: List all credit cards in the system, along with their owner's name. Include cards without a registered customer.
explain: Preserves all credit cards. Place credit cards on the left of a LEFT JOIN.

q: Show all customers and all credit cards, including unmatched records on both sides.
explain: Preserves both tables entirely, requiring a FULL OUTER JOIN.

q: Match students and courses on the condition that they are in the same department. Return all student-course pairs.
explain: Standard inner join matching on shared department code. Returns only active matches.

q: Generate all possible course-student combinations for an enrollment survey.
explain: A CROSS JOIN yields the complete Cartesian product of students and courses.

q: List all student records, along with their exam grades. Include students who missed exams.
explain: Preserves all students. Grade fields for missing exams show as NULL. Requires a LEFT JOIN.

q: Show only student records that have registered exam grades.
explain: Omit students without grades. Requires a standard INNER JOIN.

q: List all exam grades and the students they belong to. Include grades that have no matching student name in the system.
explain: Preserves all grades. Place grades on the left of a LEFT JOIN.

q: Show all students and all exam grades, displaying unmatched students and orphaned grades.
explain: Preserves both tables fully, requiring a FULL OUTER JOIN.

q: Find students who scored the exact same grade in a course. Exclude matching a student with themselves.
explain: Self-join on course_id and grade. The ID filter filters out self-matching and duplicate records.

q: List all posts and their authors. Include posts written by deleted authors (author is NULL).
explain: Preserves all posts, making it a LEFT JOIN starting with posts.

q: List all authors and the posts they have written. Include authors who have never written a post.
explain: Preserves all users/authors, making it a LEFT JOIN starting with users.

q: Show all users and all posts, including posts with no author and authors with no posts.
explain: Preserves both tables fully, requiring a FULL OUTER JOIN.

q: Find posts and their authors, excluding posts with no author and authors with no posts.
explain: Only return strict matches, requiring a standard INNER JOIN.

q: Generate all possible author-post pairings for a user review list.
explain: Requires the complete Cartesian product, which is generated via CROSS JOIN.

q: Find duplicate users who signed up using the same email address.
explain: Self-join on email. The ID filter handles self-match exclusions.

q: List all divisions and their stores. Include empty divisions with no stores.
explain: Keeps all divisions. Division table is on the left of a LEFT JOIN.

q: List all stores and their divisions. Include stores that are not assigned to a division.
explain: Keeps all stores. Store table is on the left of a LEFT JOIN.

q: Show all divisions and all stores, displaying unmatched divisions and stores together.
explain: Preserves both tables fully, requiring a FULL OUTER JOIN.

q: Find only stores that belong to a division.
explain: Strict matching between stores and divisions. Requires a standard INNER JOIN.

q: Generate all possible pairs of stores to run comparative sales checks.
sql:
SELECT s1.store_name AS store1, s2.store_name AS store2
FROM stores s1
CROSS JOIN stores s2
WHERE s1.id < s2.id;
explain: CROSS JOIN generates all store combinations. Filter removes self-pairing and duplicates.

q: Find stores located in the same city. Exclude matching a store to itself.
explain: Self-join on city. Filters eliminate self-matching and duplicate comparisons.

q: List all transactions and their payment methods. If a payment method is missing, still display the transaction details.
explain: All transactions must be preserved. Transactions with missing/unmatched payment methods will show NULL, requiring a LEFT JOIN.
